title: Patterns of Urbanisation and Economic Development, Evidence from household surveys in Ethiopia
markdown:
By **Mekdim D. Regassa**, [@Mekdim12533064] (https://twitter.com/Mekdim12533064), 2022 

**_Topic:**_ Urbanisation  

**_Keywords:**_ Africa ; Urbanisation; Cities; Towns; Household Surveys; Ethiopia 

**_Africapolis data:**_ GIS,  agglomeration data

**_Link to the original article:**_ [Patterns of Urbanisation and Economic Development, Evidence from household surveys in Ethiopia] (https://bonndoc.ulb.uni-bonn.de/xmlui/bitstream/handle/20.500.11811/9806/6666.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y)


**_Abstract:**_

*In this piece, researcher Mekdim D. Regassa illustrates the different ways in which the Africapolis dataset was used to assess how urbanization determines the spatial pattern of economic development in sub-Saharan Africa.*

###Objectives and challenges

Broadly, my research aims to assess **how urbanisation determines the spatial pattern of economic development in sub-Saharan Africa**. Specifically, it investigates if and how **proximity** to and the **size of urban areas** influences **household welfare** in the region with data from **Ethiopia**. 

One of the main challenges in such studies is the **lack** of an objective, robust, and disaggregated **measure of the degree and dynamics of urbanization**. Conventionally, measurements and definitions of urbanisation rely on survey - and census - based aggregate rural-urban indicators. However, besides often being subjective, these **indicators tend to reflect political and bureaucratic dispositions rather than the services the spaces provide**. As a result, they cannot adequately capture the enormous heterogeneities among the rural-urban areas and the rapid dynamics of urbanization (Henderson, 2003; Satterthwaite & Tacoli, 2003). To circumvent this problem, researchers have attempted to use several **alternative indicators of urbanisation** including, inter alia, population size, population density, and index of infrastructure and market access, night-time light. While these are encouraging developments, **they lack standardization to make informative comparison across time and space**.  

The data from _Africapolis_ bridges this gap by **providing disaggregated and standardized indicators of urbanization in Africa** that could also be spatially and intertemporarily matched with other datasets. In my research, the database is used in three main ways. 

###Growth of urban areas in Ethiopia

First, I used it to show the **growth of urban areas in Ethiopia** over time and space. *Figure 1* shows that, in 1960, there were only **11 urban areas with more than 10 000 inhabitants** in the country, accommodating less than 5% of the population. Over the subsequent **50 years, the number of these urban areas increased to 510** and the **share of the population** residing in such urban areas jumped to **27%** *(Table 1)*. It is not only that the number of urban areas has increased, but the **size of the existing ones has also expanded**. For instance, between **2000 and 2015** alone, the **population of Addis Ababa**, the capital city, more than **doubled**. 

###Proliferation of small and intermediate towns in Ethiopia

Second, I used the database to **demonstrate the proliferation of small and intermediate towns in Ethiopia**. *Table 1* shows the **evolution of the settlement pattern of the population across the hierarchy of cities**. It indicates that while **urbanisation** has increased, it has **increased faster in small and medium-sized towns**. Until 1990, Addis Ababa was the only large city with a population number larger than 100 000. However, by 2015, 24 other urban centres exceeded this threshold *(Panel B)*. While the capital city accounted for about **72 percent of the total urban population in 1950**, this share has continuously been declining to reach only **15.3 percent in 2015**. The accelerating growth of the urban population in general and the deceleration in the share of the capital city in the total urban population implies that the growth of other urban areas has been much faster.

######*Source: Authors’ Computation based on data from [Africapolis] (https://africapolis.org/)* 

Third, I used the _Africapolis_ dataset to **triangulate and verify the other indicators of urbanisation**; particularly indicators based on the night-time light (NTL) data. In my research, I used NTL as a main indicator of urbanization and urban dynamics. While this indicator has several advantages (e.g., availability at micro level, comparability across space), it is susceptible to measurement problems (Zhang & Seto, 2011). I, therefore, used the **Africapolis database as an additional check to verify agglomerations**. Specifically, I first geographically linked the _Africapolis_ agglomeration with the NTL database and the household survey data. Then, I cross-checked if the size of the urban agglomerations matches across the three datasets and made the necessary adjustments when these did not align.  

###References 

*Henderson, V. (2003). The urbanization process and economic growth: The so-what question. Journal of Economic Growth, 8(1), 47–71. https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1022860800744* 

*Satterthwaite, D., & Tacoli, C. (2003). The urban part of rural development: the role of small and intermediate urban centres in rural and regional development and poverty reduction (Issue May).* 

*Zhang, Q., & Seto, K. C. (2011). Mapping urbanization dynamics at regional and global scales using multi-temporal DMSP/OLS night-time light data. Remote Sensing of Environment, 115(9), 2320–2329. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2011.04.032* 

 
###*Author's Biography* 

Mekdim Regassa is a postdoctoral researcher at _Leibniz Institute for Vegetable and Ornamental Plant Production (IGZ)_. He holds a Ph.D. in agricultural economics from the University of Bonn, Germany. His research interests include econometric methods for policy evaluation, child health and nutrition, agricultural technology, and poverty and household welfare dynamics.
